channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Colorado Governor Censured for Commuting Sentence of Election Denier
item.description: The Colorado State Democratic Party, reflecting the anger of rank-and-file Democratic voters, rebuked Gov. Jared Polis on Wednesday over his decision to release Tina Peters from prison.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Michael Ciaglo for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The man is wildly out of touch with the party he effectively leads.” Gov. Jared Polis of Colorado, a Democrat, infuriated the party faithful when he commuted the prison sentence of an election denier.

item.title: A $5 Million Donation From Big Tobacco Preceded F.D.A. Vape Decision
item.description: Reynolds American’s contribution to MAGA Inc. came about one week before the Trump administration announced a new policy on e-cigarettes the company had sought.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth P. Vogel and Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Stephanie Keith/Getty Images
item.media:description: The F.D.A. has issued new guidance that could pave the way for major tobacco companies including Reynolds American to begin selling flavored vapes.

item.title: Nimitz Aircraft Carrier Enters Caribbean as Trump Pressures Cuba
item.description: The carrier arrived in the southern Caribbean on the same day that the Justice Department announced charges against Raul Castro.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Pablo Porciuncula/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The aircraft carrier U.S.S. Nimitz in Guanabara Bay in Rio de Janeiro, earlier this month.

item.title: Trump’s Government Moves to Spare an Unhappy Taxpayer Named Trump
item.description: No president has ever used the federal government to advance his own personal interests and those of his family and allies as expansively and openly as Mr. Trump has.
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump seems even less inhibited by the rules, written or unwritten, that governed his predecessors.

item.title: Judge Rules That White House Officials Must Follow Presidential Records Act
item.description: The judge overruled a government memo from April, which rejected a records law that mandates preservation of all official presidential documents.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The White House had issued guidance in April suggesting that officials did not need to preserve some of their text messages.

item.title: Trump’s Coast Guard Address: ‘Good Looking Men’ and Deja Vu
item.description: President Trump’s commencement address was a mixture of jokes and praise for the seafaring life of the Coast Guard graduates. But there were detours into choppy waters.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald J. Trump at the U.S. Coast Guard Academy in New London, Conn., on Wednesday. It was the second time he delivered the commencement address as commander in chief.

item.title: Senate G.O.P. Ready to Drop Ballroom Funds From ICE Bill
item.description: Several Republicans had balked at including the money in an immigration enforcement measure that the party plans to push through on a party-line vote.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump gave reporters a tour Tuesday of the White House ballroom construction project.

item.title: How the $1.8 Billion Trump Fund May Violate Past Practice and Policy
item.description: The fund that could offer payouts to Trump allies who accuse the government of mistreatment is not only highly unusual but also appears to violate the administration’s own policies.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: On Pam Bondi’s first day as attorney general in February 2025, she signed a directive that appeared to prohibit the kind of fund the Trump administration established this week.

item.title: Thomas Massie, a Trump Critic, Causes Latest Midterm Fight Over Israel
item.description: In primary races across the country, debates over Israel are taking center stage, dividing Democrats and Republicans alike.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Lerer and Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Jon Cherry/Getty Images
item.media:description: “I would have come out sooner, but I had to call my opponent and concede, and it took a while to find Ed Gallrein in Tel Aviv,” Representative Thomas Massie said in his concession speech on Tuesday night, referring to his Republican opponent.

item.title: Robert Woodson, Leader of the Black Conservative Movement, Dies at 89
item.description: A veteran community activist, he believed self-reliance — rather than affirmative action or government help — was the way to fight racism, poverty and crime.
item.dc:creator: Robert D. McFadden
item.media:credit: Woodson Center
item.media:description: Robert Woodson in an undated photo. As the founding president of the National Center for Neighborhood Enterprise (now the Woodson Center), he created hundreds of programs in blighted areas across the United States.

item.title: Ebola Containment Efforts May Have Been Hindered by USAID Shutdown and CDC Cuts
item.description: Aid cuts by the Trump administration have shut down crucial disease surveillance networks and medical supply chains in East Africa.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Stephanie Nolen
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Jospin Mwisha/Agence France-Presse via Getty Images
item.media:description: Monitoring visitors in Goma, in the Democratic Republic of Congo, where Ebola cases have been confirmed, on Monday.

item.title: Trump Gets His Payback in Primary Elections, but It Comes at a Cost in Congress
item.description: Republican senators are angry the president is working to unseat their colleagues. But he is also creating more free agents in his own party in Congress willing to defy him.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: After losing his primary race to a Trump-backed challenger, Senator Bill Cassidy, Republican of Louisiana, voted with Democrats in support of a measure to limit the war in Iran.

item.title: Cuba is out of gas, prices are soaring and electricity is scarce.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Adalberto Roque/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A blackout in Havana in March.

item.title: Prosecutor Is Charged With Stealing Sealed Report on Trump Documents Case
item.description: Prosecutors accused Carmen Lineberger of emailing records related to Jack Smith’s investigation to a personal account, charging her with a felony punishable with 20 years in prison.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (Documents Case)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: In the indictment, prosecutors said Carmen Lineberger emailed an internal memorandum and a report — an apparent reference to the report by Jack Smith — to a personal address.

item.title: Rubio Moves Closer to Decades-Long Goal of Transforming Cuba
item.description: The secretary of state is the son of immigrants who left Cuba before the Castro revolution to look for economic opportunities. Yet, he pursues change with the zeal of a political exile.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Marco Rubio, the secretary of state and a former senator, is a first-generation American citizen whose parents immigrated from Cuba.

item.title: 1996 Downing of Planes by Cuba Followed Months of Diplomatic Wrangling
item.description: Cuban government and the Clinton administration held several meetings discussing the volunteer group’s flights.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Alan Diaz/Associated Press
item.media:description: A plane with the Brothers to the Rescue organization flying north of Havana in 1999.

item.title: Castro Indictment Is Trump’s Latest Move Against Cuba
item.description: For President Trump, the interest in Cuba is not new.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and Secretary of State Marco Rubio have escalated a pressure campaign on the Cuban government.

item.title: How Rubio Is Driving the U.S. Pressure Campaign on Cuba
item.description: Our diplomatic correspondent Michael Crowley explains how Secretary of State Marco Rubio, who has long lobbied for an end to the regime in Cuba, is ramping up pressure on the island.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley, Nikolay Nikolov, Alexandra Ostasiewicz, Jon Miller and Whitney Shefte

item.title: Raúl Castro Has Guided Cuba for Decades
item.description: Raúl Castro is 94 and no longer holds any official title, but he still wields enormous power, experts say, particularly over the military.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams
item.media:credit: Charles Tasnadi/Associated Press
item.media:description: Fidel Castro, left, in 1986 after the Cuban Communist Party re-elected him as president, with his brother Raúl, who was re-elected as vice president.

item.title: House Passes Housing Bill, Uniting on a Measure to Bring Down Costs
item.description: The legislation, which had been stalled amid Republican divisions, passed overwhelmingly, signaling an eagerness in both parties to address affordability in an election year.
item.dc:creator: Ronda Kaysen
item.media:credit: Sean Rayford for The New York Times
item.media:description: The first major housing legislation in 36 years passed in the House, 396 to 13, on Wednesday.

item.title: Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund Tests Constitutional Limits
item.description: The deal the president reached with his own subordinates relies on a mechanism created by Congress that legal experts had warned was subject to manipulation.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The addendum to the federal government’s settlement with President Trump flirted with a grave question: Can the president pardon himself?

item.title: All the Republicans Trump Has Taken Down in Midterm Primaries
item.description: The president has spent a great deal of political capital this spring trying to unseat fellow Republicans he sees as disloyal, and he has succeeded.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Aj Mast/Associated Press
item.media:description: President Trump’s first conquest this month was helping to defeat five Indiana state lawmakers who had opposed his effort to draw new congressional maps in the state.

item.title: Jan. 6 Police Officers Sue to Block Trump’s Payout Fund
item.description: The two officers accused the administration of creating a “slush fund” to reward rioters and groups that committed violence on behalf of President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston for The New York Times
item.media:description: Officer Daniel Hodges of Washington’s Metropolitan Police, left, and Harry Dunn, then an officer with the U.S. Capitol Police, at a congressional hearing in 2022. The two filed suit on Wednesday.

item.title: Rubio, in Video, Urges Cubans to Align With Trump Administration
item.description: In a rare direct address, Secretary of State Marco Rubio blamed the former leader Raúl Castro for the country’s longstanding electricity and resource shortages.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio delivered a message directed to the Cuban people.

item.title: Barney Frank, Gay Pioneer and Liberal Stalwart in Congress, Dies at 86
item.description: Often voted the “brainiest,” “funniest” and “most eloquent” member of the House, he was also the first to come out voluntarily and helped normalize being openly gay in public office.
item.dc:creator: Katharine Q. Seelye
item.category: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (2010)

item.title: Candidates Backed by Trump, Ocasio-Cortez Triumph in Key House Primaries
item.description: The president’s preferred candidate ousted an incumbent in Kentucky, while a Democratic primary in Pennsylvania was a win for the democratic socialists.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kruse
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ed Gallrein, a farmer and former Navy SEAL, defeated Representative Thomas Massie on in the Republican primary race in Kentucky’s Fourth Congressional District.

item.title: Prison to Pardons to Payouts: Jan. 6 Rioters Are Elated at Trump’s $1.8 Billion Fund
item.description: The possibility that people who ransacked the Capitol could get money from the government they attacked is the latest head-spinning twist in President Trump’s effort to rewrite the history of Jan. 6.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston for The New York Times
item.media:description: The pro-Trump mob at the Capitol in 2021. If all of the Jan. 6ers sought money from the fund and received the same amount, the payouts would be around $1.125 million each.

item.title: With Massie’s Primary Defeat in Kentucky, Trump’s Revenge Tour Continues. But Headwinds Loom.
item.description: He has used his sway with his base to oust wayward Republicans more than he has made inroads with the independents his party needs to defeat Democrats in November.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Michael Swensen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Massie, center, after his concession speech on Tuesday in Hebron, Ky. Mr. Massie, one of President Trump’s most outspoken critics in Congress, was ousted by a primary challenger.

item.title: Raffensperger Loses in Primary for Georgia Governor
item.description: In his campaign for Georgia governor, Mr. Raffensperger found that G.O.P. voters still blamed him for Mr. Trump’s 2020 loss.
item.dc:creator: Richard Fausset and Nick Corasaniti
item.category: Georgia Criminal Case Against Trump (2020 Election)
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brad Raffensperger’s refusal to bow to President Trump’s demands in 2020 hurt him with the state’s Republican voters.

item.title: How to Get a Pardon in Trump’s Washington
item.description: Fast-talking lawyers and lobbyists promise to get white-collar criminals out of jail — for a fee.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Toobin

item.title: For the Obama Center, Mark Bradford Paints a Fierce and Luminous Chicago
item.description: “City of the Big Shoulders” reflects the city’s strength and complexity. It took the artist five years to complete, mapping migration patterns and structural racism.
item.dc:creator: Elly Fishman
item.category: Obama, Barack H, Presidential Library and Museum
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: The painter Mark Bradford stands at the edge of Lake Michigan on Chicago’s South Side. The artist drew inspiration from both the Great Lake and the city’s industrial roots for his new work commissioned by the Obama Foundation.
